pattern: Treat the beautiful-string constraint as digit validity in a positional counter, and find the next valid value by incrementing with carry, then greedily filling the freed suffix.
hints: Think of the string as a k-ary counter where each 'digit' is a letter -- finding the next larger beautiful string is finding the next value of that counter that is still valid.

Try to bump the last character up to the smallest letter greater than it that doesn't equal either of its two preceding characters; if no such letter exists within the alphabet, that position overflows and you must retry one position to the left.

Once a position accepts a bump without overflowing, every position to its right is free again -- refill each of them with the smallest letter that avoids its own two preceding characters, greedily left to right.

edge_inputs.description: statement example 1, a simple single-position bump
statement example 2, impossible because the alphabet is too small to bump past
minimal length (n=1), smallest letter bumps to the next letter
minimal length (n=1), already the largest letter of the alphabet so no answer exists
s is already the lexicographically maximal beautiful string for this length and k, so the carry cascades through every position and no answer exists
the last position must skip two forbidden candidates before finding a valid one, with no carry needed
a straightforward last-position increment with no cascade

stress.description: a random valid beautiful string of a random length is built by greedily excluding the two forbidden preceding letters at each position
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(4, 26)
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    letters = [chr(ord('a') + i) for i in range(k)]
    s = []
    for i in range(n):
        choices = [c for c in letters if not (i >= 1 and c == s[i - 1]) and not (i >= 2 and c == s[i - 2])]
        s.append(rng.choice(choices))
    return [''.join(s), k]
stress.note: construction guarantees the generated s is itself beautiful, matching the problem's precondition
